subsystem: Exiv2 PNG metadata parser. This is Exiv2::PngChunk::parseTXTChunk, the routine that decodes the three flavours of PNG textual chunk — zTXt (zlib-compressed Latin-1), tEXt (uncompressed Latin-1), and iTXt (compressed or uncompressed UTF-8) — extracting the text payload from a chunk's data buffer and returning it as a DataBuf. It is a leaf parser in the PNG image-reading path.
provenance: Identified purely by naming and API. The method is qualified PngChunk::parseTXTChunk and the trailing comment says '// PngChunk::parsePngChunk'. It uses Exiv2-specific types and symbols: DataBuf with members pData_ and size_, the byte typedef, TxtChunkType with enumerators zTXt_Chunk/tEXt_Chunk/iTXt_Chunk, the helper zlibUncompress, the enforce() macro, and error codes Error(kerFailedToReadImageData) and Exiv2::kerCorruptedMetadata. These are all from the Exiv2 library (src/pngchunk_int.cpp). The sample tag '/* sample: primevul_train_3250_vuln */' is metadata, not part of the code, and was not used to judge the code.
trust_boundary: The DataBuf 'data' is the raw payload of a PNG text chunk read from an image file (or network stream) — fully attacker-controlled: both data.pData_ (the bytes) and data.size_ (the chunk length taken from the PNG chunk header) are hostile. 'keysize' is the length of the chunk's keyword, computed by the caller from the same untrusted bytes (typically by scanning for the NUL that terminates the keyword), so it too is attacker-influenced and is used here as an unvalidated offset into data.pData_. 'type' selects the branch. The security-critical invariant that the whole function must uphold — and never checks — is that keysize plus each fixed structural offset (1, 2, or 3 bytes) stays strictly within data.size_. Nothing bounds keysize against data.size_, so every pointer computed as data.pData_ + keysize + N and every length computed as data.size_ - keysize - N is unsafe.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point. It receives the chunk payload 'data' by const reference (a DataBuf exposing a byte* pData_ and a length size_, here treated as signed long), an int 'keysize' that is the byte length of the chunk keyword the caller already parsed, and a 'type' enum selecting which PNG text-chunk format to decode. A local DataBuf 'arr' is default-constructed to hold and return the extracted text. No parameter is validated: there is no check that keysize is non-negative, that keysize is smaller than data.size_, or that data.size_ is large enough to contain the structural bytes each branch will read at fixed offsets past keysize.

The zTXt branch. A zTXt chunk is laid out as keyword, NUL separator, one compression-method byte, then the zlib stream. The code forms a pointer to the compression-method byte at data.pData_ + keysize + 1 and immediately dereferences it, requiring the byte to be 0x00 (zlib/deflate); any other value throws. The pointer arithmetic and the dereference on line 14 assume that offset keysize+1 lies inside the buffer, but that is never verified against data.size_.

The compressed payload is taken to start at data.pData_ + keysize + 2 (just past the compression-method byte), and its length is computed as data.size_ - keysize - 2. That length is stored in an unsigned int and handed, together with the pointer, to zlibUncompress, which inflates compressedTextSize bytes starting at compressedText into arr. The size arithmetic is the security core of this branch: it presumes data.size_ >= keysize + 2, which is never checked.

The tEXt branch handles uncompressed Latin-1 text: the layout is keyword, NUL, then raw text with no compression byte. The text pointer is data.pData_ + keysize + 1 and its length is data.size_ - keysize - 1, held in a signed long 'textsize'. A DataBuf is constructed from (pointer, length), which copies 'textsize' bytes out of data into arr. Correctness depends on data.size_ >= keysize + 1; there is no guard, and because textsize is signed it can go negative rather than merely wrapping.

The iTXt branch. An iTXt chunk is keyword, NUL, compression-flag byte, compression-method byte, then a NUL-terminated language tag, a NUL-terminated translated keyword, then the text. Here the code counts the NUL bytes in the half-open range [&data.pData_[keysize+3], &data.pData_[data.size_]) and enforces that at least two are present — a sanity check meant to guarantee the two NUL terminators for the language tag and translated keyword exist before the string decoding below. The range endpoints are themselves computed from untrusted keysize and data.size_: the begin pointer is data.pData_ + keysize + 3 and the end pointer is data.pData_ + data.size_.

The compression flag (0 = uncompressed, 1 = compressed) and compression method (must be 0) are read at offsets keysize+1 and keysize+2 and validated via enforce(). These indexed reads assume keysize+1 and keysize+2 are inside the buffer. The enforce checks constrain the byte *values* but do nothing to validate that the *indices* are within data.size_.

Two C-string std::string constructions. languageText is built from the bytes starting at data.pData_ + keysize + 3, scanning forward until a NUL terminator; its length gives languageTextSize. translatedKeyText then starts one byte past that NUL, at keysize + 3 + languageTextSize + 1, and again scans to a NUL, giving translatedKeyTextSize. These scans are bounded only by the presence of a NUL byte; the earlier enforce(nullSeparators >= 2) is what is meant to guarantee such NULs exist within the buffer — but only if the line-41 count range was itself valid.

Uncompressed iTXt path. The text is located just past both NUL-terminated strings, at offset keysize + 3 + languageTextSize + 1 + translatedKeyTextSize + 1, and its size is data.size_ minus that same offset, held in signed long textsize. arr.alloc(textsize) reserves a buffer and then DataBuf(text, textsize) copies textsize bytes from 'text'. Safety requires that offset to be <= data.size_ so textsize is non-negative and 'text' points within the buffer; nothing enforces it.

Compressed iTXt path (flag 1, method 0). Same offset arithmetic: compressedText points past the two strings and compressedTextSize is data.size_ minus that offset (signed long), then zlibUncompress inflates that many bytes into arr. As with the uncompressed path, the offset can exceed data.size_, making compressedTextSize negative and the pointer point past the end of the buffer.

The trailing else inside iTXt rejects any compression-flag/method combination not already handled. The outer else rejects any 'type' that is not one of the three known chunk kinds. On success the function returns arr, the extracted (and possibly inflated) text buffer, to the caller. The error paths only cover value validation, not the bounds problems established in the branches above.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
unsigned int compressedTextSize = data.size_  - keysize - 2;

            zlibUncompress(compressedText, compressedTextSize, arr);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Unsigned integer underflow into a giant length. data.size_ and keysize are both attacker-controlled; if data.size_ < keysize + 2 (a short/truncated zTXt chunk, e.g. a chunk whose whole payload is smaller than keyword+NUL+method-byte), the subtraction data.size_ - keysize - 2 goes negative and, being assigned to an unsigned int, wraps to a value near 4 GiB. That enormous compressedTextSize is passed to zlibUncompress, which will read far past the end of data.pData_ as it feeds the inflate stream — a massive out-of-bounds read (heap over-read), leading to crash or information disclosure, and potentially worse inside zlib. The invariant the implementation must uphold is: reject the chunk when data.size_ < keysize + 2 BEFORE computing the size, and compute the size with a signed/checked type. This is the central, most severe defect.

Invalid iterator range / out-of-bounds scan. std::count requires begin <= end. The begin pointer is &data.pData_[keysize+3] and the end pointer is &data.pData_[data.size_]. If keysize + 3 > data.size_ (again a short chunk relative to keysize), begin is greater than end. std::count then increments 'first' expecting to reach 'last', but it never will — it walks forward through unrelated heap memory reading each byte until it either crashes on an unmapped page or coincidentally hits the wrapped-around pointer, an unbounded out-of-bounds read and undefined behaviour. Note this guard was clearly added to bound the string decoding below, yet it is itself unbounded because its own endpoints are unvalidated. The invariant: verify keysize + 3 <= data.size_ (and keysize >= 0) before forming this range.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
const byte* compressionMethod = data.pData_ + keysize + 1;
            if ( *compressionMethod != 0x00 )
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Out-of-bounds read of the compression-method byte. If keysize + 1 >= data.size_ (a zTXt chunk that ends right after the keyword/NUL), the dereference *compressionMethod on line 14 reads one byte past the payload. On its own this is a 1-byte over-read (crash or minor info leak), but it also gates the far more dangerous size arithmetic that follows in the same branch. The invariant: confirm keysize + 1 < data.size_ before dereferencing. The same class of single-byte over-read applies to the iTXt indexed reads at lines 47 and 49 (data.pData_[keysize + 1] and [keysize + 2]) when keysize+1/keysize+2 fall outside the buffer.

Unbounded C-string scans past the buffer end. Both std::string constructors read from a raw byte pointer until they encounter a NUL. They are only safe if a NUL exists within data before the buffer ends. The line-41 enforce(nullSeparators >= 2) is intended to provide that guarantee, but it does so ONLY when the line-41 range was valid (keysize+3 <= data.size_); when it is not, or when keysize itself is bogus, these constructors scan off the end of the heap allocation — an out-of-bounds read whose length is attacker-influenced (up to the next NUL in memory). Additionally translatedKeyText starts at keysize + 3 + languageTextSize + 1, an offset that can already be past data.size_ even when two NULs existed earlier. The invariant: every string must start within the buffer and be proven NUL-terminated inside [pData_, pData_+size_).

what_could_go_wrong.code:
long textsize    = data.size_  - keysize - 1;

            arr = DataBuf(text, textsize);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Signed-underflow / negative-length copy in the tEXt branch. When data.size_ < keysize + 1, textsize becomes negative. DataBuf(text, textsize) copies 'textsize' bytes; the DataBuf constructor takes the length as a size-like value, so a negative long is converted to a huge size_t inside the memcpy, producing an enormous out-of-bounds read from 'text' (which itself may already point past the buffer). Even a merely-too-large positive textsize (short chunk but keysize computed a bit small) over-reads by the shortfall. The invariant: reject when data.size_ < keysize + 1 and treat the length as unsigned only after that check.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
long textsize    = data.size_ - (keysize + 3 + languageTextSize + 1 + translatedKeyTextSize + 1);

                arr.alloc(textsize);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Negative/oversized length in the iTXt paths. The offset keysize + 3 + languageTextSize + 1 + translatedKeyTextSize + 1 is the sum of an attacker-controlled base and two attacker-controlled string lengths; nothing proves it is <= data.size_. When it exceeds data.size_, textsize (and the identical compressedTextSize at line 84) goes negative. arr.alloc(textsize) then receives a negative long converted to a near-SIZE_MAX allocation request (denial of service / bad_alloc), and DataBuf(text, textsize) at line 73 / zlibUncompress at line 86 read from a 'text'/'compressedText' pointer that lies past the buffer with a wrapped length — out-of-bounds read/over-read. The invariant: compute the trailing-data offset, verify it is <= data.size_, and only then derive a non-negative length.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
DataBuf PngChunk::parseTXTChunk(const DataBuf& data,
                                    int            keysize,
                                    TxtChunkType   type)
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Root cause / missing input validation. 'keysize' is a signed int passed in from the caller's keyword scan and is used throughout as an unchecked offset into data.pData_, while data.size_ is the untrusted chunk length. The function never establishes the relationship keysize >= 0 and keysize + <fixed-offset> <= data.size_ that every branch silently assumes. A negative keysize would make pointers point before the buffer; a keysize near or beyond data.size_ drives all of the over-reads and underflows above. The single fix that neutralises the whole class is an up-front guard validating keysize against data.size_ for the minimum structural size of each chunk type before any pointer or length is computed.

summary: Attacker-controlled PNG text chunks flow into parseTXTChunk with no validation that the keyword length (keysize) fits inside the chunk payload (data.size_). The result is a family of out-of-bounds reads: an unsigned underflow producing a ~4 GiB length fed to zlibUncompress (zTXt, line 25), an invalid std::count range that scans off the heap (iTXt, line 41), unbounded C-string scans (lines 55/58), and signed-underflow negative lengths driving DataBuf copies and allocations (lines 35, 70, 84). This is a classic image-parser heap over-read reachable from any file/stream carrying a malformed PNG. High/critical severity.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: parseTXTChunk(const DataBuf& data, int keysize, TxtChunkType type) signature (lines 2-4)
reviewed_anchors.reason: keysize is an unchecked offset into data and is never validated against data.size_ — the root cause of every over-read.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: DataBuf data (pData_ buffer + size_ length field)
reviewed_anchors.reason: size_ is the untrusted chunk length driving all size subtractions; pData_ is the untrusted buffer indexed at keysize+N.
signed, attacker-influenced, unbounded above and below; used as raw pointer offset in every branch.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: TxtChunkType type / zTXt_Chunk, tEXt_Chunk, iTXt_Chunk enum
reviewed_anchors.reason: selector only; the outer else rejects unknown values, so the enum itself carries no memory-safety contract.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: zTXt: const byte* compressionMethod = data.pData_ + keysize + 1 (line 13) and *compressionMethod (line 14)
reviewed_anchors.reason: unbounded pointer + dereference at keysize+1 → 1-byte OOB read.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: zTXt: unsigned int compressedTextSize = data.size_ - keysize - 2 (line 25)
reviewed_anchors.reason: unsigned underflow to ~4 GiB when data.size_ < keysize+2; central heap over-read primitive.
consumes the underflowed length; the sink for the OOB read (grouped with line 25 finding).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: tEXt: long textsize = data.size_ - keysize - 1 (line 35) and DataBuf(text, textsize) (line 37)
reviewed_anchors.reason: signed underflow to negative → huge size_t copy in DataBuf constructor.
begin>end when keysize+3>data.size_ → unbounded OOB scan; the guard is itself unbounded.
the intended bound for the string scans, but only sound if line 41's range is valid; dispositioned via the line-41 and line-55 findings.
indexed reads at unvalidated offsets → 1-byte OOB reads (grouped with the line 13-14 read finding).
validates byte values only, not indices; no independent contract beyond the reads at 47/49.
unbounded C-string scan; can read past buffer end when NUL guarantee fails.
start offset can already exceed data.size_; unbounded scan (grouped with line 55 finding).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: iTXt uncompressed: long textsize = data.size_ - (keysize+3+languageTextSize+1+translatedKeyTextSize+1) (line 70), arr.alloc(textsize) (line 72), DataBuf(text, textsize) (line 73)
reviewed_anchors.reason: attacker-summed offset can exceed data.size_ → negative length → huge alloc and OOB copy.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: iTXt compressed: long compressedTextSize = data.size_ - (...) (line 84), zlibUncompress(compressedText, compressedTextSize, arr) (line 86)
reviewed_anchors.reason: same negative-length/OOB hazard as line 70 path (grouped with the line-70 finding).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: DataBuf constructor DataBuf(ptr, len) and arr.alloc(len)
reviewed_anchors.reason: implementation not shown; treated as a faithful memcpy/allocate sink — the defect is the caller passing an underflowed/negative length, captured in the length findings.
not shown; reviewed as a sink that reads 'size' bytes from 'ptr' — its danger here is the caller-supplied underflowed size, captured in the line-25 and line-84 findings.
control-flow/error reporting only; no memory contract.

findings.title: zTXt compressed-size unsigned underflow drives ~4 GiB out-of-bounds read in zlibUncompress
findings.code:
unsigned int compressedTextSize = data.size_  - keysize - 2;

            zlibUncompress(compressedText, compressedTextSize, arr);
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read via unsigned integer underflow (huge length to decompressor)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — the read occurs inside zlibUncompress, called here
findings.invariant: Before computing compressedTextSize, reject the chunk when data.size_ < keysize + 2; keep the length signed/checked so it can never wrap.
findings.failure_mode: A malformed zTXt chunk with data.size_ < keysize + 2 makes data.size_ - keysize - 2 negative; assignment to unsigned int wraps it to ~4,294,967,295. zlibUncompress then reads that many bytes starting at data.pData_ + keysize + 2, running far past the heap buffer — remote heap over-read causing crash or information disclosure from adjacent memory.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm data.size_ is a signed long that can be smaller than keysize+2, that compressedTextSize is unsigned (32-bit) as declared, and that zlibUncompress uses the length as an inflate input bound without its own clamp.

findings.title: iTXt NUL-count uses an invalid iterator range (begin > end) enabling an unbounded heap over-read
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read via invalid range (begin pointer past end pointer)
findings.invariant: Verify keysize >= 0 and keysize + 3 <= data.size_ so the [begin,end) range is well-formed before calling std::count.
findings.failure_mode: When keysize + 3 > data.size_ the begin pointer &data.pData_[keysize+3] exceeds the end pointer &data.pData_[data.size_]. std::count advances first until it equals last, which never happens for a begin-past-end range, so it walks forward through unrelated memory reading each byte until it faults or wraps — undefined behaviour and an unbounded out-of-bounds read. Ironically this guard was added to bound the string decoding below yet is itself unbounded.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no caller-side check guarantees keysize+3 <= data.size_, and that data.size_ is the untrusted chunk length.

findings.title: iTXt trailing-data length underflow: negative size feeds arr.alloc, DataBuf copy, and zlibUncompress
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read / oversized allocation via signed length underflow
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — DataBuf::alloc and the DataBuf(ptr,len) copy; same pattern at line 84/86 for zlibUncompress
findings.invariant: Compute the trailing offset keysize+3+languageTextSize+1+translatedKeyTextSize+1, verify it is <= data.size_, and only then derive a non-negative length; treat alloc/copy sizes as unsigned only after the check.
findings.failure_mode: languageTextSize and translatedKeyTextSize are attacker-controlled string lengths; their sum plus keysize can exceed data.size_, making textsize (and the identical compressedTextSize at line 84) negative. arr.alloc(textsize) then converts the negative long to a near-SIZE_MAX request (allocation failure / DoS), and DataBuf(text, textsize) at line 73 (or zlibUncompress at line 86) copies/reads from a 'text' pointer already past the buffer with a wrapped length — heap out-of-bounds read.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm DataBuf::alloc and the DataBuf constructor treat the long length as size_t without rejecting negatives, and that no bound relates the two string sizes to data.size_.

findings.title: tEXt text length underflow yields negative size passed to DataBuf copy
findings.code:
long textsize    = data.size_  - keysize - 1;

            arr = DataBuf(text, textsize);
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read via signed length underflow / missing short-buffer guard
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — the copy inside the DataBuf(ptr,len) constructor
findings.invariant: Reject the chunk when data.size_ < keysize + 1 before computing textsize; do not pass a negative length to DataBuf.
findings.failure_mode: A tEXt chunk whose payload is shorter than keyword+NUL (data.size_ < keysize + 1) makes textsize negative; DataBuf(text, textsize) converts it to a huge size_t inside memcpy, copying enormous amounts from 'text' (itself possibly past the buffer) — heap over-read, crash or disclosure.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the DataBuf constructor's length parameter is size-like and performs a memcpy without validating sign, so a negative long becomes a huge copy.

findings.title: Unbounded C-string scans for language tag and translated keyword can read past buffer end
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (unbounded NUL-terminated scan of untrusted buffer)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0008_cwe125.cpp (scan performed by std::string's char* constructor)
findings.invariant: Both string starts must lie within [pData_, pData_+size_) and a NUL must be guaranteed to occur before pData_+size_; do not rely on a guard (line 41) whose own range may be invalid.
findings.failure_mode: The char* std::string constructors scan until a NUL. The line-41 enforce is meant to guarantee the two NULs, but only holds when its range was valid (keysize+3 <= data.size_). If keysize is bogus or the count range under-flowed, or if translatedKeyText's start (keysize+3+languageTextSize+1) already sits at/after data.size_, the scan runs off the end of the allocation reading attacker-length bytes into a std::string — out-of-bounds read.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm there is no independent bound tying keysize+3 and the language-tag start to data.size_, and that the line-41 guard is the only thing standing between these constructors and the buffer end.

findings.title: One-byte out-of-bounds reads of compression flag/method bytes at unvalidated offsets
findings.anchor: const byte* compressionMethod = data.pData_ + keysize + 1; if ( *compressionMethod != 0x00 )
findings.code:
const byte* compressionMethod = data.pData_ + keysize + 1;
            if ( *compressionMethod != 0x00 )
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (single byte at unchecked offset)
findings.invariant: Confirm keysize + 1 < data.size_ (and, for iTXt, keysize + 2 < data.size_) before reading the compression-method/flag bytes.
findings.failure_mode: A chunk that ends immediately after the keyword/NUL (data.size_ <= keysize + 1) causes *compressionMethod to read one byte past the buffer. The same pattern applies to the iTXt indexed reads data.pData_[keysize + 1] (line 47) and data.pData_[keysize + 2] (line 49). Impact is a crash or minor info leak, and these reads also gate the more severe size arithmetic in the same branches.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no caller guarantees data.size_ > keysize + 2 for iTXt / > keysize + 1 for zTXt.

findings.title: Missing validation of keysize against data.size_ (root cause of all over-reads)
findings.anchor: DataBuf PngChunk::parseTXTChunk(const DataBuf& data, int keysize, TxtChunkType type)
findings.code:
DataBuf PngChunk::parseTXTChunk(const DataBuf& data,
                                    int            keysize,
                                    TxtChunkType   type)
findings.bug_class: Missing input validation (untrusted offset vs buffer length)
findings.invariant: On entry, require keysize >= 0 and data.size_ >= keysize + (minimum structural bytes for 'type'); reject otherwise before any pointer/length is computed.
findings.failure_mode: keysize is a signed offset derived from the same untrusted bytes as data; nothing checks keysize >= 0 or keysize + fixed-offset <= data.size_. A negative keysize points before the buffer; an oversized keysize drives every downstream underflow and over-read. A single entry guard neutralises the whole class.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the caller (parseChunkContent) does not already bound keysize to the chunk length, and how keysize is derived (keyword NUL scan).

audit_checklist: Add an entry guard: keysize >= 0 and data.size_ >= keysize + minimum-structural-size for the selected chunk type, before any pointer/length arithmetic.
zTXt line 25: reject when data.size_ < keysize + 2 before computing compressedTextSize; never let the length wrap in an unsigned type.
iTXt line 41: validate keysize + 3 <= data.size_ so the std::count [begin,end) range is well-formed (begin <= end).
iTXt lines 70/84: verify keysize+3+languageTextSize+1+translatedKeyTextSize+1 <= data.size_ so textsize/compressedTextSize are non-negative before alloc/copy/inflate.
tEXt line 35: reject when data.size_ < keysize + 1 so textsize is non-negative before DataBuf(text, textsize).
iTXt lines 55/58: bound the language-tag and translated-keyword scans to the buffer (e.g. use lengths from the validated NUL positions) rather than raw char* std::string construction.
Confirm DataBuf::alloc and the DataBuf(ptr,len) constructor reject negative/oversized lengths instead of converting to size_t and memcpy'ing.
Confirm zlibUncompress clamps or validates its input length against the real buffer size rather than trusting the caller.
Check whether data.size_ is signed long vs unsigned and audit every 'data.size_ - keysize - N' subtraction for sign/underflow consistency.
